era_desc: Rise of mammals and flowering plants.
period_desc: Repeated ice ages locked huge volumes of water into continental ice sheets, repeatedly dropping sea levels and exposing land bridges between continents that let animals and eventually humans migrate into new territory. As the ice sheets advanced and retreated in cycles, grasslands and tundra spread across regions that had been forest, favoring large grazing mammals adapted to open, cold landscapes over the browsing species that came before them. Modern humans emerged during this period and, through hunting and later farming, became a new kind of force reshaping which other species could survive alongside them.
period_imgDesc: Woolly mammoths — classic Ice Age reconstruction painting
epoch_desc: As the last ice age ended, retreating glaciers raised sea levels and ushered in a stable, warm interglacial climate that let forests and grasslands recolonize the land. That climatic stability gave early humans, who had survived for millennia as nomadic hunter-gatherers, a predictable growing season, and this predictability drove the domestication of wild grasses and animals into the first crops and livestock. Farming produced surplus food, which let people settle permanently, build villages, and free up labor for specialized crafts, laying the foundations of civilization. Within just the last two centuries, that same civilization began burning stored carbon at a rate that is now itself the dominant force reshaping global climate.
epoch_imgDesc: Çatalhöyük — Neolithic farming settlement
period_flora: Woolly mammoths, giant ground sloths, cave lions and saber toothed cats roamed alongside early Homo sapiens.
period_atmo: Oxygen and CO2 near modern levels, though CO2 dipped lower during glacial peaks.
period_temp: Cycled repeatedly between deep ice ages and warmer interglacial periods like today.
period_earth: Continents sat in roughly their modern positions; ice sheets rather than plate motion drove most environmental change.
epoch_flora: Domesticated einkorn wheat and barley, along with sheep and cattle descended from wild aurochs, sustained early farming villages like Çatalhöyük.
epoch_atmo: Atmospheric CO2 held near 280 ppm for most of the epoch before human industry pushed it past 420 ppm in just the last two centuries.
epoch_temp: A stable, mild interglacial climate prevailed for most of the epoch, though the last century has seen rapid, human-driven warming.
epoch_earth: Sea levels rose over 120 meters as ice sheets melted, drowning land bridges like Doggerland and the Bering Strait crossing and reshaping coastlines into their modern outlines.

period_imgDesc: Woolly mammoths — classic Ice Age reconstruction painting
epoch_desc: Cyclical wobbles in Earth's orbit locked the planet into repeated ice ages, as vast ice sheets advanced and retreated across the northern continents roughly every hundred thousand years. Each glaciation opened huge, cold, dry grasslands, the mammoth steppe, that favored large, fur covered grazers and the big predators that hunted them, while lowered sea levels exposed land bridges that let animals and early humans wander between continents. Hunting and toolmaking grew steadily more sophisticated among early human populations, letting them spread out of Africa and into nearly every landmass exposed by the ice age. When the last glaciation ended and the climate warmed rapidly, shrinking grasslands combined with pressure from increasingly effective human hunters to drive most of the era's giant mammals extinct.
epoch_imgDesc: Woolly mammoths, rhinos, horses, and cave lions roamed.
period_flora: Woolly mammoths, giant ground sloths, cave lions and saber toothed cats roamed alongside early Homo sapiens.
period_atmo: Oxygen and CO2 near modern levels, though CO2 dipped lower during glacial peaks.
period_temp: Cycled repeatedly between deep ice ages and warmer interglacial periods like today.
period_earth: Continents sat in roughly their modern positions; ice sheets rather than plate motion drove most environmental change.
epoch_flora: Woolly mammoths, woolly rhinoceroses, cave lions, and wild horses roamed the mammoth steppe grasslands.
epoch_atmo: CO2 oscillated between roughly 180 ppm during glacial maxima and 280 ppm in warm interludes, well below today's levels throughout.
epoch_temp: Temperatures swung repeatedly between deep glacial cold and brief warm interglacials, averaging colder than today's climate overall.
epoch_earth: Falling sea levels during glacial peaks exposed the Bering land bridge and the Sunda Shelf in Southeast Asia, opening migration routes between continents that are submerged today.

era_desc: Rise of mammals and flowering plants.
period_desc: Grasslands expanded across the cooling, drying continents as forests retreated, and that spread of open grazing land rewarded mammals that could run fast on long legs and grind tough grasses with high crowned teeth. Those pressures split many lineages into distinct grazing and browsing forms and, in Africa, pushed one group of apes toward walking upright on two legs to cover open ground efficiently. Deep ocean channels also connected and closed as continents shifted, letting marine mammals spread into new oceans while isolating others.
period_imgDesc: Megatherium, giant ground sloth, life reconstruction
epoch_desc: Global cooling and drying, intensified as the rising Isthmus of Panama rerouted ocean currents and dried out Africa, caused dense forests to shrink into scattered patches of open savanna and woodland. That spreading grassland put a premium on primates able to travel efficiently between shrinking food sources on the ground, favoring early hominins that walked upright over ones still built for climbing. Freed from supporting locomotion, their hands could grip and manipulate objects with increasing precision, and by the epoch's final million years some hominin groups began deliberately flaking stone into simple cutting tools. That combination of upright walking and toolmaking set the stage for the rapid brain growth and technological change that would follow in the next epoch.
epoch_imgDesc: Australopithecus anamensis — earliest known hominin
period_flora: Giant ground sloths, early horses, and the first upright walking hominins like Australopithecus appeared.
period_atmo: Oxygen and CO2 approaching modern levels as global cooling continued.
period_temp: Steadily cooling and drying, ending the warm greenhouse conditions of earlier periods.
period_earth: The Isthmus of Panama began rising, and the Himalayas continued growing as India pushed into Asia.
epoch_flora: Australopithecus anamensis and its later descendant Australopithecus afarensis walked the African savanna alongside saber-toothed cats like Homotherium.
epoch_atmo: CO2 started near 400 ppm, comparable to today, then gradually declined through the epoch as global cooling took hold.
epoch_temp: Climate began mild and warm, a few degrees above today's average, before cooling sharply in the epoch's final stretch as ice ages approached.
epoch_earth: The Isthmus of Panama rose fully out of the sea, closing the gap between the Atlantic and Pacific and letting land animals cross between North and South America for the first time in tens of millions of years.

era_desc: Rise of mammals and flowering plants.
period_desc: Grasslands expanded across the cooling, drying continents as forests retreated, and that spread of open grazing land rewarded mammals that could run fast on long legs and grind tough grasses with high crowned teeth. Those pressures split many lineages into distinct grazing and browsing forms and, in Africa, pushed one group of apes toward walking upright on two legs to cover open ground efficiently. period_imgDesc: Megatherium, giant ground sloth, life reconstruction
epoch_desc: Continued global cooling and the ongoing collision of India into Asia pushed the Himalayas and Tibetan Plateau ever higher, intensifying monsoon patterns that dried out much of the interior and let fast growing, abrasive grasses spread across the continents. Grazing mammals responded by evolving taller, harder teeth suited to grinding down tough grass, thriving as open grassland replaced forest across large parts of Africa and Eurasia. As forests fragmented into isolated patches, tree dwelling apes diversified into many species adapted to whatever woodland remained, radiating widely across Africa, Europe, and Asia. Late in the epoch, further shrinking and splitting of African forest habitat isolated one ape population from another, setting them down separate evolutionary paths that would eventually lead to humans and chimpanzees.
epoch_imgDesc: Apes evolved and diversified, with the human–chimp lineage splitting late.
period_flora: Giant ground sloths, early horses, and the first upright walking hominins like Australopithecus appeared.
period_atmo: Oxygen and CO2 approaching modern levels as global cooling continued.
period_temp: Steadily cooling and drying, ending the warm greenhouse conditions of earlier periods.
period_earth: The Isthmus of Panama began rising, and the Himalayas continued growing as India pushed into Asia.
epoch_flora: Ancestral apes like Proconsul, Sivapithecus, and Dryopithecus diversified widely across Africa and Eurasia.
epoch_atmo: CO2 declined gradually across the epoch, from around 400 ppm early on to near modern pre-industrial levels by its end.
epoch_temp: An early warm phase, the Miocene Climatic Optimum, gave way to steady cooling and drying through the rest of the epoch.
epoch_earth: Continued collision between India and Asia kept lifting the Himalayas and Tibetan Plateau, while at the epoch's very end the closing Strait of Gibraltar dried the Mediterranean Sea into a vast salt basin during the Messinian Salinity Crisis.

era_desc: Rise of mammals and flowering plants.
period_desc: With the dinosaurs gone, small surviving mammals suddenly found forests and open ground free of large predators and competitors, and within a few million years they radiated into an enormous range of new forms, from early primates to the ancestors of whales and horses. Warm, humid greenhouse conditions early in the period let dense forests spread even near the poles, but a shift toward a cooler, drier climate later on began carving those forests back into open woodland. Birds, too, briefly grew into giant flightless predators before mammals took over the top predator role for good.
period_imgDesc: Andrewsarchus mongoliensis, giant mammalian predator, restoration
epoch_desc: As Antarctica finally drifted free of South America, the Drake Passage opened and let a continuous current encircle the pole, thermally isolating Antarctica from warmer waters further north. That isolation let a permanent ice sheet build up on Antarctica for the first time, and the resulting global cooling dried out continental interiors, shrinking dense forests into more open woodland and early grassland. Cooler, more seasonal conditions favored mammals with more adaptable teeth and larger brains over the primitive, big jawed carnivorous mammals that had dominated since the dinosaurs died out. Those older specialist hunters gradually died out and were replaced by more versatile early carnivore groups better suited to the changing, more open landscape.
epoch_imgDesc: Dogs, nimravids, bears, weasels, and raccoons replaced the creodonts.
period_flora: Early primates, the giant predatory bird Gastornis, and Andrewsarchus, the largest land predator ever, all thrived.
period_atmo: CO2 started far above modern levels, then declined sharply as the period wore on.
period_temp: Began in an extreme warm hothouse phase, then cooled steadily through the rest of the period.
period_earth: India collided with Asia partway through the period, beginning to push up the Himalayas.
epoch_flora: Early dog-like Hesperocyon, saber-toothed Nimravus, and the first true bears and weasels spread as forests opened into grassland.
epoch_atmo: CO2 fell sharply across the epoch, dropping from roughly 750 ppm to near 450 ppm, still well above today's level.
epoch_temp: Global temperatures dropped sharply at the epoch's start, the first time since the dinosaur extinction that permanent ice built up anywhere on Earth.
epoch_earth: The opening of the Drake Passage between South America and Antarctica, along with a widening Tasmanian Gateway, completed the Southern Ocean's circumpolar current and sealed Antarctica into permanent isolation.

period_imgDesc: Andrewsarchus mongoliensis, giant mammalian predator, restoration
epoch_desc: A massive, rapid release of carbon at the epoch's start drove the Paleocene-Eocene Thermal Maximum, spiking temperatures and turning much of the planet, even near the poles, into warm, dense rainforest. That lush, continuous greenhouse world let land bridges across the North Atlantic connect North America and Europe, allowing early hoofed mammals to spread and radiate rapidly into the many new forest niches such warmth created. As those hoofed lineages diversified into browsers, runners, and swimmers, some even ventured back into shallow coastal waters, setting one lineage on its evolutionary path from land mammal to ocean dweller. Toward the epoch's end, slow removal of atmospheric carbon by rock weathering and organic burial finally began cooling the planet, setting up the sharp chill that would follow in the next epoch.
epoch_imgDesc: Hoofed animals diversified in a major radiation between Europe and North America.
period_flora: Early primates, the giant predatory bird Gastornis, and Andrewsarchus, the largest land predator ever, all thrived.
period_atmo: CO2 started far above modern levels, then declined sharply as the period wore on.
period_temp: Began in an extreme warm hothouse phase, then cooled steadily through the rest of the period.
period_earth: India collided with Asia partway through the period, beginning to push up the Himalayas.
epoch_flora: Small early horses like Eohippus and early whales such as Basilosaurus radiated as forests spread across a warm planet.
epoch_atmo: CO2 surged past 1000 ppm during the Paleocene-Eocene Thermal Maximum and stayed several times higher than today for most of the epoch.
epoch_temp: This was the warmest sustained stretch of the entire Cenozoic era, with even polar regions warm enough to support crocodiles and palm trees.
epoch_earth: Land bridges across the North Atlantic via Greenland still linked North America and Europe before finally rifting apart, while India's northward drift brought it into its first collision with Asia partway through the epoch.

era_desc: Rise of mammals and flowering plants.
period_desc: With the dinosaurs gone, small surviving mammals suddenly found forests and open ground free of large predators and competitors, and within a few million years they radiated into an enormous range of new forms, from early primates to the ancestors of whales and horses. Birds, too, briefly grew into giant flightless predators before mammals took over the top predator role for good.
period_imgDesc: Andrewsarchus mongoliensis, giant mammalian predator, restoration
epoch_desc: The asteroid impact that ended the age of dinosaurs left most of the planet's large ecological niches suddenly empty, while small, burrowing, generalist mammals that had survived the disaster on scraps of insects and seeds were left to inherit the wreckage. With almost no competition from large predators or herbivores, those surviving mammal lineages diversified rapidly, growing larger and specializing into new diets and habitats within just a few million years. As forests recovered from the destruction, some mammals moved into the trees to exploit fruit, insects, and shelter above the ground, adapting grasping hands and forward facing eyes suited to life in the branches. That arboreal lifestyle produced the earliest primates, setting mammals on a path of diversification that would define the rest of the Cenozoic.
epoch_imgDesc: Purgatorius — earliest known primate
period_flora: Early primates, the giant predatory bird Gastornis, and Andrewsarchus, the largest land predator ever, all thrived.
period_atmo: CO2 started far above modern levels, then declined sharply as the period wore on.
period_temp: Began in an extreme warm hothouse phase, then cooled steadily through the rest of the period.
period_earth: India collided with Asia partway through the period, beginning to push up the Himalayas.
epoch_flora: Purgatorius, the earliest known primate, foraged in the recovering forests alongside early hoofed mammals like Phenacodus.
epoch_atmo: CO2 remained elevated at several times today's level, helping keep the recovering world warm.
epoch_temp: Climate warmed steadily through the epoch, building toward the extreme heat spike that would mark its very end.
epoch_earth: India, isolated as a fast moving island continent, sped northward across the shrinking Tethys Sea, while lingering volcanism from the Deccan Traps continued to alter the atmosphere in the epoch's early years.

era_desc: Age of dinosaurs. First birds appear.
period_desc: Flowering plants evolved and spread rapidly, replacing the ferns and conifers that had dominated for over 100 million years, and their fast growth, fruit, and nectar drove a burst of co-evolution, fueling the rise of pollinating insects and diversifying plant eating dinosaurs and the predators that hunted them into the most varied ecosystem the Mesozoic had ever seen. That entire web collapsed in an instant when a large asteroid struck the Yucatan 66 million years ago, blotting out the sun long enough to kill off the plants at the base of the food chain and, with them, every dinosaur that could not fly.
period_imgCredit: Charles R. Knight, c. 1928 — Public domain
period_imgDesc: Triceratops confronting Tyrannosaurus — iconic Late Cretaceous scene
epoch_desc: Flowering plants continued spreading across the landscape, supporting new insect pollinators and offering fresh food sources that let plant eating dinosaurs diversify into a wide range of specialized grazers and browsers. This abundance of prey in turn supported some of the largest land predators to ever evolve, while horned and duck billed dinosaur groups reached their greatest diversity worldwide. Then a massive asteroid struck the Earth, throwing debris and dust into the atmosphere that blocked sunlight, collapsed food chains, and abruptly ended the reign of the non avian dinosaurs alongside most other life on the planet.
epoch_imgDesc: K–Pg extinction wiped out ~75–80% of species, including all non-avian dinosaurs.
period_flora: Flowering magnolias and early oaks spread alongside Triceratops, duck billed hadrosaurs, and Tyrannosaurus.
period_atmo: CO2 several times higher than today; oxygen around 28-30%.
period_temp: Warm greenhouse climate, with no polar ice caps for most of the period.
period_earth: Pangaea's breakup continued as the Atlantic widened, splitting continents into isolated landmasses that let dinosaur groups evolve independently region by region.
epoch_flora: Tyrannosaurus, Triceratops, Ankylosaurus, and Hadrosaurus flourished right up until the mass extinction ended their reign.
epoch_atmo: CO2 stood several times higher than today until the impact's aftermath filled the skies with dust and disrupted the carbon cycle.
epoch_temp: Warm greenhouse conditions prevailed for most of the epoch, followed by a sharp, sudden post impact cooling at its very end.
epoch_earth: A massive asteroid struck the Yucatan Peninsula while the Deccan Traps in India were simultaneously erupting enormous lava flows.

era_desc: Age of dinosaurs. First birds appear.
period_desc: Flowering plants evolved and spread rapidly, replacing the ferns and conifers that had dominated for over 100 million years, and their fast growth, fruit, and nectar drove a burst of co-evolution, fueling the rise of pollinating insects and diversifying plant eating dinosaurs and the predators that hunted them into the most varied ecosystem the Mesozoic had ever seen. That entire web collapsed in an instant when a large asteroid struck the Yucatan 66 million years ago, blotting out the sun long enough to kill off the plants at the base of the food chain and, with them, every dinosaur that could not fly.
period_imgCredit: Charles R. Knight, c. 1928 — Public domain
period_imgDesc: Triceratops confronting Tyrannosaurus — iconic Late Cretaceous scene
epoch_desc: The first flowering plants appeared and began to spread, introducing new sources of nectar, fruit, and foliage that reshaped plant communities and encouraged insects to diversify alongside them. As river deltas and shallow seas teemed with fish, some large predatory dinosaurs evolved long narrow snouts and semi aquatic habits to exploit this abundant food source, while horned plant eating dinosaurs and small feathered hunters radiated into many new forms across the changing landscape. Continents now clearly separated by widening seaways allowed many of these groups to evolve independently in different parts of the world.
epoch_imgDesc: Spinosaurids (shown here), Ceratopsians, Carcharodontosaurids and Coelurosaurs emerged.
period_flora: Flowering magnolias and early oaks spread alongside Triceratops, duck billed hadrosaurs, and Tyrannosaurus.
period_atmo: CO2 several times higher than today; oxygen around 28-30%.
period_temp: Warm greenhouse climate, with no polar ice caps for most of the period.
period_earth: Pangaea's breakup continued as the Atlantic widened, splitting continents into isolated landmasses that let dinosaur groups evolve independently region by region.
epoch_flora: Spinosaurus, Iguanodon, Deinonychus, and the early ceratopsian Psittacosaurus thrived across different continents.
epoch_atmo: CO2 levels ran markedly higher than today, several times preindustrial concentrations.
epoch_temp: Generally warm and mild with no polar ice caps, though somewhat cooler than the preceding Jurassic.
epoch_earth: The South Atlantic began to open as South America and Africa continued rifting apart from one another.

era_desc: Age of dinosaurs. First birds appear.
period_desc: As the breakup of Pangaea opened shallow seaways between the drifting continents, sauropod dinosaurs grew to enormous sizes to browse the tall conifer and cycad forests that thrived in the warm, humid climate, while smaller predators evolved feathers, setting one lineage on a path toward true flight. Marine reptiles that had returned to the ocean diversified alongside newly abundant squid like ammonites, and by the period's end, the first true birds had taken to the air.
period_imgDesc: Brontosaurus — long-necked sauropod
epoch_desc: Vast conifer forests and fern plains provided so much foliage that sauropod dinosaurs evolved to enormous sizes in order to browse treetops that smaller herbivores could not reach, and this abundance of giant prey favored the evolution of powerful, large bodied predatory dinosaurs. In response to constant predation pressure, some plant eating dinosaurs evolved heavy body armor and defensive spikes, while a separate lineage of small meat eating dinosaurs refined feathers that had originally evolved for insulation or display into structures capable of gliding and, eventually, powered flight. By the end of the epoch, this lineage had given rise to the first true birds taking wing above the forests.
epoch_imgDesc: Theropods (shown here), Sauropods and Thyreophorans emerged.
period_flora: Giant sauropods, early feathered theropods, ichthyosaurs, and abundant ammonites filled land and sea.
period_atmo: CO2 remained well above modern levels; oxygen slightly below today's.
period_temp: Warm and humid worldwide, with lush forests reaching high latitudes.
period_earth: Pangaea actively split into northern and southern landmasses, opening new shallow seas between them.
epoch_flora: Archaeopteryx, Stegosaurus, Diplodocus, and Allosaurus all lived alongside one another.
epoch_atmo: CO2 remained well above modern levels, sustaining the lush forests that fed the giant herbivores.
epoch_temp: Warm and humid globally, with little seasonal variation and no polar ice.
epoch_earth: The Atlantic Ocean began to open as North America started separating from Africa.

era_desc: Age of dinosaurs. First birds appear.
period_desc: As the breakup of Pangaea opened shallow seaways between the drifting continents, sauropod dinosaurs grew to enormous sizes to browse the tall conifer and cycad forests that thrived in the warm, humid climate, while smaller predators evolved feathers, setting one lineage on a path toward true flight. Marine reptiles that had returned to the ocean diversified alongside newly abundant squid like ammonites, and by the period's end, the first true birds had taken to the air.
period_imgDesc: Brontosaurus — long-necked sauropod
epoch_desc: As Pangaea continued to break apart, newly formed shallow seas and changing rainfall patterns created a wider variety of habitats than had existed before, and early long necked sauropods took advantage of this by growing larger and spreading across the emerging continents. Large carnivorous dinosaurs evolved in step to hunt these bigger herbivores, while early plant eating ornithopods began exploring a more agile, two legged alternative to the giant sauropod body plan. Meanwhile, small early mammals avoided competition with the daytime dominant dinosaurs by specializing for a nocturnal lifestyle, allowing them to diversify quietly into a range of new forms alongside the reptiles.
epoch_imgDesc: Brachiosaurs (shown here), Cetiosaurs, Megalosaurs and primitive Ornithopods emerged.
period_flora: Giant sauropods, early feathered theropods, ichthyosaurs, and abundant ammonites filled land and sea.
period_atmo: CO2 remained well above modern levels; oxygen slightly below today's.
period_temp: Warm and humid worldwide, with lush forests reaching high latitudes.
period_earth: Pangaea actively split into northern and southern landmasses, opening new shallow seas between them.
epoch_flora: Brachiosaurus, Cetiosaurus, Megalosaurus, and early ornithopods like Callovosaurus roamed the changing continents.
epoch_atmo: CO2 stayed well above today's levels, several times the preindustrial norm.
epoch_temp: Warm greenhouse climate throughout, generally milder than the following Late Jurassic.
epoch_earth: Pangaea's breakup accelerated as the Tethys Sea widened between its northern and southern landmasses.

era_desc: Age of dinosaurs. First birds appear.
period_desc: As the breakup of Pangaea opened shallow seaways between the drifting continents, sauropod dinosaurs grew to enormous sizes to browse the tall conifer and cycad forests that thrived in the warm, humid climate, while smaller predators evolved feathers, setting one lineage on a path toward true flight. Marine reptiles that had returned to the ocean diversified alongside newly abundant squid like ammonites, and by the period's end, the first true birds had taken to the air.
period_imgDesc: Brontosaurus — long-necked sauropod
epoch_desc: With many of their rivals wiped out by the extinction that closed the Triassic, dinosaurs quickly diversified to fill the empty ecological niches left on land, growing in size and variety across the reunited landmass. In the oceans, ammonites that had nearly vanished rebounded explosively, providing abundant prey that encouraged marine reptiles to diversify and refine sleek, fast swimming body forms. On land, early mammals remained small, but freed from many former competitors, they began quietly diversifying into a wider range of forms alongside the rapidly evolving dinosaurs.
epoch_imgDesc: Ammonites explosively diversified after near-extinction.
period_flora: Giant sauropods, early feathered theropods, ichthyosaurs, and abundant ammonites filled land and sea.
period_atmo: CO2 remained well above modern levels; oxygen slightly below today's.
period_temp: Warm and humid worldwide, with lush forests reaching high latitudes.
period_earth: Pangaea actively split into northern and southern landmasses, opening new shallow seas between them.
epoch_flora: Ichthyosaurs, Dimorphodon, and the early sauropodomorph Massospondylus thrived alongside explosively diversifying ammonites.
epoch_atmo: CO2 remained elevated compared to today, though somewhat lower than the extinction era spike that preceded it.
epoch_temp: Warm and humid worldwide, recovering steadily from the severe climate disruption of the end Triassic extinction.
epoch_earth: Pangaea remained largely intact, though early rifting had begun to stretch and weaken its interior.

era_desc: Age of dinosaurs. First birds appear.
period_desc: Life had to rebuild almost from scratch after the largest extinction in Earth's history, and in that emptied world a group of small reptiles evolved into the first dinosaurs while another lineage of reptiles independently evolved fur, warm bloodedness, and other traits that would eventually define mammals. Recovering marine life, freed from established competitors, quickly diversified into new reef building and swimming forms across the world's single vast ocean.
period_imgDesc: Plateosaurus — early large herbivore
epoch_desc: In the hot, arid climate that dominated much of the supercontinent, a group of small, agile, bipedal reptiles evolved into the first true dinosaurs, competing alongside a range of other reptile groups for dominance on land. As Pangaea began to stretch and crack, enormous volcanic eruptions released huge volumes of gas into the atmosphere, triggering rapid climate swings and ocean acidification. This upheaval culminated in a mass extinction that wiped out many of the dinosaurs' rival reptile groups, clearing the way for dinosaurs to inherit the emptied world in the epoch that followed.
epoch_imgDesc: Herrerasaurus (shown here), Plateosaurus, Coelophysis, and Eoraptor emerged.
period_flora: Early dinosaurs, the mammal ancestor group cynodonts, and early ichthyosaurs emerged alongside conifer forests.
period_atmo: CO2 far above modern levels in the aftermath of the Permian extinction.
period_temp: Extremely hot early on, gradually cooling to a warm, arid climate.
period_earth: All continents were locked together in the single supercontinent Pangaea, surrounded by one world ocean.
epoch_flora: Herrerasaurus, Plateosaurus, Coelophysis, and Eoraptor represented the first wave of true dinosaurs.
epoch_atmo: CO2 spiked sharply toward the epoch's end as intensifying volcanic outgassing took hold.
epoch_temp: Hot and arid overall, growing increasingly unstable as the epoch's mass extinction approached.
epoch_earth: Rifting within Pangaea produced the massive volcanic province that would trigger the end Triassic extinction.

era_desc: Age of dinosaurs. First birds appear.
period_desc: Life had to rebuild almost from scratch after the largest extinction in Earth's history, and in that emptied world a group of small reptiles evolved into the first dinosaurs while another lineage of reptiles independently evolved fur, warm bloodedness, and other traits that would eventually define mammals. Recovering marine life, freed from established competitors, quickly diversified into new reef building and swimming forms across the world's single vast ocean.
period_imgDesc: Plateosaurus — early large herbivore
epoch_desc: As life continued its slow recovery from the devastation of the Permian extinction, reptiles that had returned to the sea found abundant open niches and began diversifying into an array of new swimming forms, gradually evolving more streamlined bodies suited to hunting in open water. On land, early archosaurs and other reptile groups continued to diversify and compete for dominance, setting the stage for the dinosaurs that would soon emerge. Meanwhile, the vast interior of Pangaea began to slowly stretch and drift, opening shallow seas along its margins that provided rich new habitat for these diversifying marine reptiles.
epoch_imgDesc: Mixosaurus panxianensis — early ichthyosaur.
period_flora: Early dinosaurs, the mammal ancestor group cynodonts, and early ichthyosaurs emerged alongside conifer forests.
period_atmo: CO2 far above modern levels in the aftermath of the Permian extinction.
period_temp: Extremely hot early on, gradually cooling to a warm, arid climate.
period_earth: All continents were locked together in the single supercontinent Pangaea, surrounded by one world ocean.
epoch_flora: Mixosaurus, Nothosaurus, and the early archosaur Ticinosuchus populated the shallow seas and coasts.
epoch_atmo: CO2 remained elevated above modern levels as the post extinction recovery continued.
epoch_temp: Warm throughout, gradually stabilizing after the extreme heat of the Early Triassic.
epoch_earth: Pangaea began slowly drifting and stretching, opening shallow marginal seas favorable to marine reptiles.

period_desc: Life had to rebuild almost from scratch after the largest extinction in Earth's history, and in that emptied world a group of small reptiles evolved into the first dinosaurs while another lineage of reptiles independently evolved fur, warm bloodedness, and other traits that would eventually define mammals. Recovering marine life, freed from established competitors, quickly diversified into new reef building and swimming forms across the world's single vast ocean.
period_imgDesc: Plateosaurus — early large herbivore
epoch_desc: In the aftermath of the most severe mass extinction in Earth's history, surviving reptiles faced almost no competition and began radiating rapidly to fill the emptied ecological niches left on land. Among these survivors, one reptile lineage evolved into the first archosaurs, a group that would later split into separate branches leading toward crocodiles on one side and dinosaurs on the other. Harsh, extremely hot conditions in the extinction's immediate wake slowed the pace of recovery at first, but as the climate gradually stabilized, life diversified more quickly across the single vast landmass.
epoch_imgDesc: Erythrosuchus — one of the first archosauriforms.
period_flora: Early dinosaurs, the mammal ancestor group cynodonts, and early ichthyosaurs emerged alongside conifer forests.
period_atmo: CO2 far above modern levels in the aftermath of the Permian extinction.
period_temp: Extremely hot early on, gradually cooling to a warm, arid climate.
period_earth: All continents were locked together in the single supercontinent Pangaea, surrounded by one world ocean.
epoch_flora: Erythrosuchus, Lystrosaurus, and Proterosuchus dominated the recovering landscape.
epoch_atmo: CO2 stood extremely high, a lingering result of the volcanism that drove the Permian extinction.
epoch_temp: Extremely hot at first, among the harshest climates of the Mesozoic, before gradually easing later in the epoch.
epoch_earth: All continents remained fused into the single supercontinent Pangaea, surrounded by the world ocean Panthalassa.

era_desc: Explosion of complex life. Sea to land.
period_desc: With the continents fused into a single supercontinent, vast desert interiors formed far from any coastline, favoring reptiles whose waterproof eggs and scaly skin let them thrive away from water in a way amphibians never could. That advantage let synapsids, the reptile like ancestors of mammals, become the dominant land animals for tens of millions of years, until massive volcanic eruptions flooded the atmosphere with greenhouse gases and acidified the oceans, wiping out nearly all life on Earth in a single catastrophic event.
period_imgCredit: Marco Romano, Fabio Manucci, Bruce Rubidge & Marc J. Van den Brandt, 2021 — CC BY 4.0
period_imgDesc: Scutosaurus — armored reptile of the Permian
epoch_desc: Therapsid predators had dominated the land for tens of millions of years, but toward the close of the Permian, massive volcanic eruptions in what is now Siberia began pumping enormous volumes of greenhouse gases into the atmosphere. That surge of carbon dioxide drove runaway warming, which acidified the oceans and stripped them of oxygen, collapsing marine food webs from the bottom up. On land, the same warming dried out remaining wetlands and disrupted the plant communities that herbivores depended on, so the collapse spread up through every level of the food chain. The result was the most severe extinction event in Earth's history, wiping out nearly all species on the planet within a geologically short span.
epoch_imgDesc: Gorgonops — top predator before extinction
period_flora: Sail backed Dimetrodon, the predator Gorgonops, and early conifer forests dominated before the extinction.
period_atmo: Oxygen fell from earlier highs; CO2 spiked sharply at the period's end from volcanic activity.
period_temp: Generally hot and arid, ending in extreme volcanic driven warming.
period_earth: All continents merged into the supercontinent Pangaea, creating vast interior deserts far from any ocean.
epoch_flora: Dicynodon and Lystrosaurus persisted among the last therapsids, while Glossopteris forests collapsed as the extinction unfolded.
epoch_atmo: CO2 spiked sharply from Siberian Traps volcanism while oxygen levels crashed to some of the lowest in Earth's history.
epoch_temp: Extreme, rapid warming driven by volcanic greenhouse gases capped an already hot Permian climate.
epoch_earth: Massive flood basalt eruptions of the Siberian Traps blanketed millions of square kilometers of Pangaea's interior.

period_imgDesc: Scutosaurus — armored reptile of the Permian
epoch_desc: As pelycosaurs declined, a new group of synapsids with more efficient jaws and upright postures diversified rapidly and rose to dominate life on land. Their success let complex terrestrial food webs form, with large predators supporting a range of smaller herbivorous and insectivorous species beneath them. Partway through the epoch, however, volcanic activity in what is now southern China triggered a sharp extinction pulse that thinned out marine and some terrestrial life, and before ecosystems fully recovered, further environmental stress pushed many lineages toward a second wave of losses. This left surviving therapsid groups to inherit depleted ecosystems heading into the following epoch.
epoch_imgDesc: Titanophoneus — top predator of the Guadalupian.
period_flora: Sail backed Dimetrodon, the predator Gorgonops, and early conifer forests dominated before the extinction.
period_atmo: Oxygen fell from earlier highs; CO2 spiked sharply at the period's end from volcanic activity.
period_temp: Generally hot and arid, ending in extreme volcanic driven warming.
period_earth: All continents merged into the supercontinent Pangaea, creating vast interior deserts far from any ocean.
epoch_flora: Anteosaurus and Estemmenosuchus stalked the land as Glossopteris seed ferns still dominated southern forests.
epoch_atmo: Oxygen levels remained comparatively high while CO2 rose during the mid-epoch volcanic pulse.
epoch_temp: Warm and generally stable, interrupted by short-lived cooling after the extinction pulses.
epoch_earth: Eruption of the Emeishan Traps in what is now southern China accompanied the epoch's extinction events.

period_imgDesc: Scutosaurus — armored reptile of the Permian
epoch_desc: Early synapsids with sail-like structures on their backs used them to regulate body temperature more efficiently than earlier land animals, letting them grow larger and forage across a wider range of habitats. This gave them a decisive edge over the amphibians that had previously dominated, and synapsids soon became the largest and most numerous land vertebrates. At the same time, insects underwent their own burst of diversification, evolving complete metamorphosis and exploiting the drying, increasingly varied landscape in ways that had not existed before. Together these changes produced far more complex terrestrial ecosystems than the swampy world that had preceded them.
epoch_imgDesc: Dimetrodon — a pelycosaur, early reptile-like synapsid.
period_flora: Sail backed Dimetrodon, the predator Gorgonops, and early conifer forests dominated before the extinction.
period_atmo: Oxygen fell from earlier highs; CO2 spiked sharply at the period's end from volcanic activity.
period_temp: Generally hot and arid, ending in extreme volcanic driven warming.
period_earth: All continents merged into the supercontinent Pangaea, creating vast interior deserts far from any ocean.
epoch_flora: Sail backed Edaphosaurus and Ophiacodon roamed alongside some of the earliest true beetles and flies.
epoch_atmo: Oxygen declined from Carboniferous highs while CO2 began a gradual rise through the epoch.
epoch_temp: Increasingly warm and arid as Pangaea's interior dried out.
epoch_earth: Collision of Baltica and Siberia raised the Ural Mountains, the very range the epoch is named after.

era_desc: Explosion of complex life. Sea to land.
period_desc: Trees evolved lignin, a tough new structural molecule that let plants grow taller than ever, but no microbe had yet evolved the ability to break lignin down, so dead trees piled up undecomposed for millions of years, eventually compressing into the coal seams that give the period its name. That buried, undecayed plant matter pushed atmospheric oxygen far above today's levels, and that oxygen rich air, in turn, let insects and arthropods, which breathe through simple tubes with no lungs, grow to enormous sizes, while the amniotic egg evolved, finally letting vertebrates reproduce fully on dry land.
period_imgDesc: Arthropleura, giant Carboniferous millipede, artist's impression
epoch_desc: Tetrapods that had until then been tied to water for reproduction evolved an egg with its own internal membranes and a protective shell, letting it survive on dry land without drying out. Freed from the need to return to ponds and streams to breed, these amniotes spread deep into drier inland habitats that earlier vertebrates could never reach. Meanwhile the coal swamp forests covering much of the tropics were pushing atmospheric oxygen to unusually high levels, which in turn allowed some invertebrates to grow to enormous sizes. Together these developments set amniotes on a path toward the sweeping land radiation that followed in the Permian.
epoch_imgDesc: Anatomy of an amniotic egg — key innovation for land reproduction.
period_flora: Giant club moss forests sheltered Meganeura, a 70cm dragonfly, and Arthropleura, a millipede over 2 meters long.
period_atmo: Oxygen peaked near 35%, the highest level in Earth's history.
period_temp: Global cooling set in, eventually tipping into a late period ice age.
period_earth: Wandering continents collided to form the supercontinent Pangaea, pushing up mountain ranges and creating the broad equatorial swamp belt where these coal forests grew.
epoch_flora: Hylonomus, among the earliest known reptiles, lived alongside giant dragonflies like Meganeura in towering Lepidodendron forests.
epoch_atmo: Oxygen peaked at levels well above today's, fueling gigantism in insects and other arthropods.
epoch_temp: Warm and humid in the tropics, with glaciation building at southern high latitudes.
epoch_earth: Repeated glacial cycles on Gondwana caused sea levels to rise and fall, alternately drowning and exposing coal swamp lowlands.

period_imgDesc: Arthropleura, giant Carboniferous millipede, artist's impression
epoch_desc: As the Carboniferous began, sea levels rose enough to flood vast stretches of the continents, turning much of the Northern Hemisphere into shallow, warm seas. Those seas provided ideal conditions for filter feeding marine animals, which multiplied so prolifically that their skeletal remains built up into thick limestone deposits across whole continents. On the fringes of these flooded lowlands, the amphibians that had recently ventured onto land found abundant wetland habitat, and their lineages diversified into a range of new forms. This spread of shallow marine and wetland environments laid the groundwork for the swampy coal forests that would dominate later in the period.
epoch_imgDesc: Fossil crinoid (sea lily) from Mississippian Indiana limestone
period_flora: Giant club moss forests sheltered Meganeura, a 70cm dragonfly, and Arthropleura, a millipede over 2 meters long.
period_atmo: Oxygen peaked near 35%, the highest level in Earth's history.
period_temp: Global cooling set in, eventually tipping into a late period ice age.
period_earth: Wandering continents collided to form the supercontinent Pangaea, pushing up mountain ranges and creating the broad equatorial swamp belt where these coal forests grew.
epoch_flora: Crinoids formed vast underwater meadows while early sharks like Stethacanthus patrolled the shallow seas, and amphibians such as Pederpes explored the wetland fringes.
epoch_atmo: Oxygen was climbing steadily while CO2 remained relatively low compared to the preceding Devonian.
epoch_temp: Warm overall, though cooling began at higher latitudes as the epoch progressed.
epoch_earth: Widespread continental flooding drowned much of Laurussia and Gondwana's margins beneath shallow tropical seas.

era_desc: Explosion of complex life. Sea to land.
period_desc: Jawed fish diversified explosively in the world's oceans, and as some lineages evolved lungs and stronger fins to cope with oxygen poor shallow waters, one group began pulling itself out onto land for short stretches at a time. Meanwhile the first forests spread across the continents, and their deep root systems broke down rock into soil at a scale never seen before, changing rivers and coastlines and setting the stage for the wave of extinctions that closed out the period.
period_imgDesc: Dunkleosteus terrelli, armored Devonian predatory fish, reconstruction
epoch_desc: Lobe finned fish living in crowded, oxygen poor shallow waters had already evolved sturdy limb-like fins, and some now began hauling themselves onto land for short stretches to escape competition and predators. This gradual shift let a fully terrestrial group of vertebrates emerge for the first time, opening up an entirely unoccupied set of habitats along rivers and coastlines. At the same time, changes in ocean chemistry, likely tied to nutrient runoff from the continents' expanding forests, triggered repeated bouts of oxygen starvation in the seas. These anoxic events devastated marine life, especially reef building organisms, and marked one of the most severe extinction intervals of the entire Paleozoic.
epoch_imgDesc: Tiktaalik — first fish to walk on land
period_flora: Armored fish like Dunkleosteus, early lobe finned fish, and the first forests of primitive trees flourished.
period_atmo: Oxygen climbed as the first forests spread, though still below Carboniferous peaks.
period_temp: Warm throughout, cooling somewhat by the period's end.
period_earth: Continents drifted toward each other, forming new mountain ranges and shrinking the shallow seas that fish dominated ecosystems relied on.
epoch_flora: Ichthyostega and Acanthostega, among the first tetrapods, emerged onto land as reef building stromatoporoids collapsed during the era's extinction events.
epoch_atmo: Oxygen continued rising from forest expansion even as marine anoxic events periodically starved the oceans.
epoch_temp: Warm through most of the epoch, with cooling pulses accompanying the extinction events.
epoch_earth: As the Acadian orogeny wound down, Gondwana began drifting toward Laurussia, setting up the collisions that would build Pangaea in the following period.

period_imgDesc: Dunkleosteus terrelli, armored Devonian predatory fish, reconstruction
epoch_desc: As vascular plants spread across the continents in growing numbers, their root systems began breaking down bare rock into true soil on a scale never seen before, and the first true forests took hold. Those forests altered rainfall and river patterns while also boosting atmospheric oxygen, changing conditions on both land and in nearby waters. In the oceans, jawed fish were diversifying rapidly, and as some populations became crowded into stagnant, oxygen poor shallow waters, they began evolving lung-like adaptations to extract oxygen directly from air. This adaptation gave certain fish lineages the physiological tools that would let their descendants eventually move onto land.
epoch_imgDesc: Dunkleosteus — armoured apex predator
period_flora: Armored fish like Dunkleosteus, early lobe finned fish, and the first forests of primitive trees flourished.
period_atmo: Oxygen climbed as the first forests spread, though still below Carboniferous peaks.
period_temp: Warm throughout, cooling somewhat by the period's end.
period_earth: Continents drifted toward each other, forming new mountain ranges and shrinking the shallow seas that fish dominated ecosystems relied on.
epoch_flora: Eusthenopteron, a lobe finned fish, swam beneath the first Archaeopteris trees alongside armored predators like Coccosteus.
epoch_atmo: Oxygen rose steadily as the first forests spread, though it remained below the peaks reached later in the Carboniferous.
epoch_temp: Warm and stable across most of the epoch.
epoch_earth: The Acadian orogeny intensified as Avalonia collided with Laurussia, raising mountains that shed sediment into the Old Red Sandstone basins.

epoch_desc: Vascular land plants evolved true roots and stems, letting them grow taller and colonize dry ground far from any water source for the first time. Their spread created shaded, sheltered habitats and organic litter that arthropods soon began exploiting, drawing whole communities of invertebrates permanently onto land alongside the plants. In the oceans meanwhile, fish with hinged jaws diversified rapidly, giving them a major advantage over jawless forms in capturing and processing prey. Alongside them, coiled shelled predators multiplied through the shallow seas, marking a new era of marine predation that would define ocean ecosystems for the rest of the period.
epoch_imgDesc: Pleuroceras solare — early Devonian ammonoid fossil.
period_flora: Armored fish like Dunkleosteus, early lobe finned fish, and the first forests of primitive trees flourished.
period_atmo: Oxygen climbed as the first forests spread, though still below Carboniferous peaks.
period_temp: Warm throughout, cooling somewhat by the period's end.
period_earth: Continents drifted toward each other, forming new mountain ranges and shrinking the shallow seas that fish dominated ecosystems relied on.
epoch_flora: Early plants like Cooksonia and Trimerophytes spread across the land as the first jawed placoderms and ammonoids diversified in the seas.
epoch_atmo: Oxygen remained close to today's levels while CO2 was substantially higher than the modern atmosphere.
epoch_temp: Warm globally, with no polar ice caps.
epoch_earth: Continued closure of the Iapetus Ocean drove the early stages of mountain building along colliding northern continents.

era_desc: Explosion of complex life. Sea to land.
period_desc: Recovering from the extinction that closed the previous period, life took advantage of newly stable, warm shallow seas to rebuild diverse coral reef ecosystems, while a thickening layer of atmospheric ozone finally made it safe for plants to leave the water and colonize damp ground near the shore. Those first simple land plants, in turn, gave small arthropods something to eat and hide in, drawing animal life onto land for the first time.
period_imgDesc: Eurypterus — Silurian sea scorpion
epoch_desc: Terrestrial ecosystems expand with plants and arthropods.
epoch_imgCredit: Muséum national d'Histoire naturelle — CC BY 3.0
period_flora: Coral reefs, sea scorpions like Eurypterus, and the first simple land plants and arthropods appeared.
period_atmo: Oxygen continued rising steadily, thickening the ozone layer enough to shield land from UV radiation.
period_temp: Warm and stable, following the end of a brief glaciation.
period_earth: Continents drifted together into smaller landmasses, closing ancient oceans and building new mountain belts.

period_imgDesc: Eurypterus — Silurian sea scorpion
epoch_desc: As sea levels stabilized and shallow seas stayed warm, coral and reef-building organisms spread rapidly across continental shelves, rebuilding the reef ecosystems that earlier Silurian recovery had only begun. Those reef frameworks created sheltered, resource-rich habitats, which in turn supported a burst of diversification among the grazers, cephalopods, and armored predators patrolling them. The resulting richness of reef life produced some of the most complex marine communities of the early Paleozoic, with predators, filter feeders, and reef builders locked in tight ecological interdependence. This reef-driven recovery marked the high point of Silurian marine life before later environmental disruptions began to erode it.
epoch_imgDesc: Silurian seafloor — cephalopods, trilobites, crinoids and corals.

epoch_flora: Halysites and Favosites corals built dense reefs alongside Eurypterus sea scorpions and Calymene trilobites.
epoch_atmo: Carbon dioxide remained several times higher than today, while oxygen crept closer to modern levels.
epoch_temp: Warm, stable greenhouse conditions prevailed across shallow tropical seas.
epoch_earth: Continued closure of the Iapetus Ocean pushed Baltica and Laurentia into collision, beginning to raise the mountain belt that would become the Caledonides.

epoch_desc: As glaciers left over from the end of the Ordovician melted, sea levels rose quickly, flooding low-lying continental shelves and opening vast new stretches of shallow, oxygen-rich habitat. That flooding gave surviving marine groups, thinned out by the preceding mass extinction, the room and stability they needed to rebound. As populations recovered, reef-building organisms recolonized the newly submerged shelves, restarting coral and reef growth that had been wiped out. This slow rebuilding set the stage for the fuller reef ecosystems that would flourish later in the Silurian.

epoch_flora: Pentamerus brachiopods, Monograptus graptolites, and early eurypterids recolonized the recovering shallow seas.
epoch_atmo: Carbon dioxide stayed far above modern levels even as post-extinction oxygen slowly recovered.
epoch_temp: Climate warmed steadily as the end-Ordovician ice sheets retreated.
epoch_earth: Rapid melting of Gondwana's ice caps drove a sharp glacio-eustatic sea-level rise that flooded continental margins worldwide.

era_desc: Explosion of complex life. Sea to land.
period_desc: An explosive diversification of marine life packed the shallow seas with far more species than the Cambrian had ever held, as filter feeders, corals, and early fish filled niches left wide open by the previous period's simpler ecosystems. That long buildup ended abruptly when shifting continents triggered a severe ice age, locking up sea water as ice, crashing sea levels, and wiping out a large share of the marine life that had just diversified.
period_imgCredit: Charles R. Knight, c. 1929 — Public domain
period_imgDesc: Ordovician sea floor with trilobites and cephalopods, Knight mural
epoch_desc: As Gondwana drifted directly over the South Pole, massive ice sheets built up across it, locking away enormous volumes of ocean water. That ice growth caused sea levels to fall sharply, draining the shallow seas most marine life depended on and collapsing habitats across the globe. Global cooling compounded the damage, killing off warmth-adapted species unable to tolerate the sudden chill. When the ice sheets later melted, a second wave of oxygen-poor water swept across the seafloor, finishing off many survivors and closing out the Ordovician in one of the largest mass extinctions in Earth's history.
epoch_imgDesc: Hirnantian glacial diamictite deposit near Porto, Portugal
period_flora: Corals, cephalopods, and early jawless fish flourished in diverse, crowded shallow seas.
period_atmo: Oxygen kept rising gradually; CO2 remained far higher than today.
period_temp: Warm for most of the period, then plunged into a sudden severe ice age at its close.
period_earth: The southern supercontinent Gondwana drifted over the South Pole, triggering the glaciation that ended the period.
epoch_flora: Cold-tolerant Hirnantia brachiopods spread widely even as Endoceras cephalopods and Isotelus trilobites dwindled.
epoch_atmo: Carbon dioxide dropped as glaciation intensified but remained well above modern concentrations.
epoch_temp: Climate swung from warm to a sudden, severe ice age.
epoch_earth: Continent-scale ice sheets centered over Gondwana, in the region that is now the Sahara, locked up water and drove the extinction-triggering sea-level collapse.

epoch_desc: Widespread volcanic activity along colliding tectonic margins pumped ash and nutrients into shallow seas that were already flooding low-lying continents as sea levels rose. Those nutrient-rich, sunlit shelf waters gave filter feeders and reef builders enormous new areas to colonize, fueling a rapid expansion of marine diversity. As new species filled the flooded shelves, competition intensified, pushing further specialization among predators, grazers, and stationary reef dwellers. This combination of flooding and volcanic disturbance helped drive the diversification that defined the middle of the Ordovician.

epoch_flora: Orthoceras cephalopods, Flexicalymene trilobites, and Prasopora bryozoans spread across the newly flooded shelf seas.
epoch_atmo: Carbon dioxide remained many times higher than today, with oxygen still below modern levels.
epoch_temp: Warm greenhouse conditions accompanied unusually high global sea levels.
epoch_earth: Subduction along Laurentia's margin triggered the onset of the Taconic mountain-building event, blanketing shallow seas in volcanic ash now preserved as widespread bentonite beds.

epoch_desc: Following the Cambrian, survivors inherited nutrient-rich, high sea-level shelf seas and began evolving new ways to filter food and build skeletons that earlier animals had not tried. Those innovations let them exploit niches the Cambrian fauna had left untouched, triggering a burst of diversification that tripled the number of marine families within a few million years. As new filter feeders, grazers, and predators filled the water column and seafloor, they built increasingly complex reef and hardground communities in place of the simpler Cambrian ecosystems. This surge of innovation, now called the Great Ordovician Biodiversification Event, reshaped marine life far beyond anything the Cambrian had produced.
epoch_imgDesc: Intact Asaphellus trilobite fossils from Early Ordovician Morocco

epoch_flora: The giant predator Cameroceras hunted among Didymograptus graptolites and early Tetradium corals.
epoch_atmo: Carbon dioxide levels stood many times above today's, while oxygen continued its slow long-term rise.
epoch_temp: Warm greenhouse climate persisted across most shallow seas.
epoch_earth: Continued rifting widened the Iapetus Ocean between Laurentia, Baltica, and Avalonia, while Gondwana began the slow poleward drift that would later trigger glaciation.

era_desc: Explosion of complex life. Sea to land.
period_desc: Rising oxygen and calcium levels in the oceans made it newly viable for animals to build hard shells and skeletons, and once a few species had armor, everything else was forced to evolve eyes, speed, or weapons of its own just to keep up, touching off an evolutionary arms race. That arms race is what produced, in barely 20 million years, nearly every major animal body plan alive today, from armored filter feeders to fast, hard shelled hunters patrolling the seafloor.
period_imgDesc: Anomalocaris — Cambrian apex predator
epoch_desc: As ocean chemistry grew unstable late in the Cambrian, periodic overturns brought oxygen-poor deep water up onto shallow shelves, repeatedly poisoning the trilobite communities living there. Each of these die-offs wiped out most trilobite lineages in a given region, only for surviving groups to rapidly diversify and refill the empty niches within a few million years. This repeating cycle of collapse and rebound played out several times, leaving marine ecosystems increasingly unstable. That instability culminated in an extinction pulse that closed out the Cambrian and cleared the way for the next period's very different marine fauna.
epoch_imgDesc: Agnostoid trilobite fossils packed in Furongian-age limestone
period_flora: Trilobites, early arthropods, and the apex predator Anomalocaris ruled warm shallow seas; land remained barren.
period_atmo: Oxygen still well below modern levels, but rising steadily.
period_temp: Warm climate following the end of a global glaciation.
period_earth: Tectonic rifting broke up the old supercontinent Pannotia, flooding the fragments with vast shallow seas that opened huge stretches of new seafloor for life to colonize.
epoch_flora: Dikelocephalus and Olenus trilobites dominated shelf seas repeatedly disrupted alongside early conodonts such as Cordylodus.
epoch_atmo: Oxygen levels fluctuated sharply while carbon dioxide stayed far higher than today.
epoch_temp: Warm overall, but punctuated by short-lived episodes of ocean chemistry disruption.
epoch_earth: Subduction along Gondwana's margin drove the early stages of the Ross-Delamerian mountain-building episode as recurring ocean overturn events triggered the biomere extinctions.
